cards.title: AI Agents Are Starving for Smart Data: Build the 'Token Whisperer' They Need
cards.thesis: AI agents are quickly becoming indispensable for builders, but they're still incredibly inefficient and expensive when it comes to gathering information from the web. They often fetch huge amounts of raw data (measured in 'tokens,' which are like chunks of text an AI processes) that are costly and full of irrelevant noise, especially from complex websites. There's a huge opportunity to build tools that act as smart data filters, feeding AI agents only the lean, relevant info they need.

evidence.text: One Wikipedia page costs your AI agent 68,000 tokens of raw HTML; Nike's homepage is 353,000. While Claude Code's built-in webfetch handles easy cases, it struggles with JavaScript-rendered and anti-bot pages.
I once had to triple app capacity and double database capacity just for one scraper. Cloudflare previously identified Perplexity as using 'stealth, undeclared crawlers' to bypass website rules.

As Claude Code, Codex, and other AI coding agents take up more and more of our daily work, I've noticed that I now spend most of my time writing specs and talking to the agent, rather than poring over code details. I haven't opened Stack Overflow in a very long time.
GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra produced a proof of the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture, showing advanced AI capabilities when given precise inputs.

cards.opportunity: AI agents like Claude Code and Cursor are becoming essential, but their web research is incredibly inefficient and expensive, often wasting 68,000 'tokens' (the units AI uses to process info) on a single Wikipedia page. Instead of general scraping, build a specialized 'smart web agent' that intelligently extracts *only the core content* from specific, high-value pages (like API docs or research papers), summarizes it, and then delivers that distilled, token-optimized data directly to an AI. Ship this as a plug-in for popular AI coding tools, and you'll immediately solve a massive cost and accuracy problem for thousands of builders.

cards.title: Devs Are Done With AI Interruptions: Build Tools That Restore Focus, Not Break It
cards.thesis: Developers are feeling overwhelmed and alienated by current AI coding tools, which they say disrupt their 'flow state' (deep, focused work) with constant interruptions and make them feel like 'glorified code reviewers.' This widespread frustration is leading to burnout and a desire for AI solutions that augment rather than dictate their work, sparking a demand for tools that prioritize developer focus and control.

evidence.text: Many developers are feeling 'alienated by LLMs,' seeing their careers 'crumble' and their identity as a software engineer questioned, feeling like they'd 'rather do anything else than become a glorified code reviewer for machine generated code.'

One developer expressed frustration with coding agents, saying 'waiting for the prompt to finish introduces constant interruptions, which translates into constant distractions, constant context switch, infinite ideas for endless new projects. My brain is melting.' They miss 'deep focus' and the ability to work in a flow state.

There's a debate about whether 'frontier models' (the most advanced AI models) are truly necessary, with many suggesting that 'open (weight) models 6 months behind the frontier are good enough for the majority of ‘work’,' indicating a potential over-reliance on cutting-edge AI when simpler solutions might suffice.

Developers with experience in common stacks like React and Node.js are struggling to find jobs, prompting them to ask if they 'should switch stacks,' which suggests a shift in required skills or a tightening job market influenced by new technologies.

cards.opportunity: Developers are feeling like 'glorified code reviewers' and are fed up with AI agents constantly interrupting their flow, making their 'brain melt.' The market is screaming for tools that restore deep work, not disrupt it. You could build a 'flow state' plugin for popular AI IDEs (integrated development environments, like Cursor) that queues AI suggestions and agent outputs silently, letting developers review them only when *they're* ready, not when the AI dictates. Ship it this weekend and own the developer sanity market.
